question_1.text: Can a plastic food container with oily residue be recycled in the blue bin?
options: A) Yes, all plastic is recyclable.
B) No, contamination ruins the batch.
question_1.explanation: Contamination from food waste ruins the recycling process. Always rinse it first!

question_2.text: Where should your family dispose of old electronic charging cables or broken tablets in Singapore?
options: A) The blue household recycling bin.
B) A specialized E-waste recycling bin (like ALBA bins).
question_2.explanation: E-waste contains heavy metals that require special handling. They should never go into the general blue bins!

question_3.text: Your family just received a grey 'Bloobox' from the NEA. What is its main purpose?
options: A) To store food waste for composting.
B) To help households sort and collect clean recyclables at home before heading to the blue bin.
question_3.explanation: The Bloobox was distributed to Singapore households to make it easy for families to separate clean recyclables right at the source!

question_4.text: You found an empty metal Milo tin. Before tossing it into the Blue Bin, what should your child do?
options: A) Ensure it is empty and give it a quick rinse.
B) Crush it and throw it in immediately with the plastic lid attached.
question_4.explanation: Leftover drink residue can attract pests and contaminate papers in the blue bin. A quick rinse keeps the recycling stream clean!

question_5.text: Which of these items CANNOT be recycled in Singapore's Blue Bin?
options: B) Used tissue papers and disposable paper cups.
question_5.explanation: Used tissues are unhygienic, and paper cups are lined with plastic waterproof coatings. Both must go to the general waste bin!

question_6.text: Singapore's only landfill, Semakau Landfill, is filling up fast! By which year is it projected to run out of space if we don't recycle right?
question_6.explanation: Semakau Landfill is expected to be completely full by 2035. Reducing waste and recycling properly helps extend its lifespan!
